cataloging_subject.label: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The intellectual and editorial unit published by Carl Horstmann in Anglia 8 (1885), pp. 102-196, and represented as CME00060: German editorial matter plus four related fifteenth-century Middle English translations and the anonymous compiler-translator's opening and closing apologies. The unit is not a single saint's life, not the entirety of MS. Douce 114, not the adjacent Seven Points of True Love, and not identical with Brown's later three-life critical edition.

catalog_summary.title: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator/compiler; Latin source authors Philip of Clairvaux, Thomas of Cantimpré, Jacques de Vitry, and Stefano Maconi
catalog_summary.date: Middle English translations produced in the first quarter of the fifteenth century; surviving Douce 114 copy from the first half of the fifteenth century; edited selection published 1885
catalog_summary.region: England; language localized to the Rutland area and broadly Central Midlands, with Beauvale, Nottinghamshire established as later monastic provenance rather than certain production

titles.value: Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114
notes: The subtitle distinguishes this article from other Horstmann publications also headed Prosalegenden.

titles.supplied_by: Horstmann's printed headline and the CME XML

titles.value: The Lives of Four Female Saints
notes: The label is convenient but imprecise: the Catherine item is an epistolary testimony, not a full conventional vita.

scope.description: Middle English translations and the translator's own framing statements

notes: The translator calls himself simply learned, says he works sense-for-sense from Latin, and states that he undertook the labor at his prior's command.

scope.description: Four-text Middle English grouping and compiler framing

notes: Brown attributes responsibility for gathering the four works to one compiler, while the surviving Catherine text may occupy a separately added quire.

scope.description: Immediate 1885 article, transcription, German introduction, headings, and notes

notes: Horstmann is the modern editor, not the medieval author or translator.

scope.description: Latin source life of Elizabeth of Spalbeek

notes: This is authorship of the Latin source work; the Middle English translator remains anonymous.
This is source authorship, not authorship of the Middle English expression.
The Middle English compiler says he omits Jacques's long figurative Latin proem and substitutes a brief notice.

scope.description: Latin letter concerning Catherine of Siena

notes: The letter is addressed to Thomas Antonii of Siena and is dated 26 October 1411; Horstmann corrects the manuscript's 1311 date.

date_statements.display: First quarter of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: This is a scholarly dating of the translations, not the dates of the Latin source works or the surviving copy.
Date of the Latin source letter. The surviving manuscript writes 1311; Horstmann corrects it to 1411 from the letter's historical context and Latin source.

date_statements.display: First half of the fifteenth century in the surviving Douce 114 grouping; the Catherine quire may have been added later

assertion.notes: Compilation and witness production are not assumed to be one event.

date_statements.display: First half of the fifteenth century in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Douce 114

assertion.notes: The current repository record gives the broader fifteenth century; the narrower range is scholarly.

date_statements.display: 1885, publication of Horstmann's four-text edited selection in Anglia 8

assertion.notes: This is the publication date of the cataloged edited selection, not a medieval work-composition date.

places.label: Rutland area
notes: Angus McIntosh's linguistic localization as reported by Vander Veen.

assertion.notes: Vander Veen describes the manuscript as largely conforming to the fifteenth-century Central Midlands written standard, while the compiler himself acknowledges northern and southern mixture.
This is a linguistic localization reported from McIntosh, not proof of translation or copying at a specific institution.

scope.description: Provenance of the manuscript witness, not the 1885 edition

assertion.notes: The ownership inscription establishes that the codex belonged to Beauvale; it does not by itself prove production or translation there.

scope.description: Horstmann's introduction, supplied headings, commentary, and notes

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Source titles, quotations, formulas, authorities, and editorial discussion embedded in German and Middle English prose

notes: The root XML LANG value names Middle English and German only, but direct inspection confirms substantive embedded Latin strings and quotations.

form_statements.description: The German editorial matter, Middle English lives, letter, and compiler apologies are prose; brief quotations do not make the unit mixed-form.

genre_statements.label: Hagiographic letter
notes: The item is an epistolary testimony about Catherine's deeds and virtues rather than a full continuous vita.

subject_statements.label: Passion devotion and embodied imitation of Christ
Miracles, visions, prophecy, and extraordinary devotion

assertion.notes: Beauvale ownership and the compiler's obedience to a prior support Carthusian reception; exact translation and production location remain unresolved.

subject_statements.label: Catherine of Siena's deeds, virtues, and canonization testimony

summaries.value: Horstmann's edited selection presents three Middle English lives of holy women from the Liège region—Elizabeth of Spalbeek, Christina Mirabilis, and Marie d'Oignies—followed by Stefano Maconi's 1411 letter about Catherine of Siena. Anonymous compiler-translator apologies frame the group and explain sense-for-sense translation, selective omission of difficult scriptural authorities, obedience to a prior, mixed northern and southern English, and several translation choices. Horstmann supplies a German codicological and source introduction and extensive notes.

The encoded article contains Horstmann's introduction; an opening compiler apology; the Life of Elizabeth with six thematic chapters; a prologue and thirty-eight chapters of Christina; a compiler prologue and two thirteen-chapter books of Marie; Maconi's letter concerning Catherine; and the compiler's closing apology. The source-text boundaries share printed pp. 134, 184, and 195.

The manuscript witness continues beyond the cataloged selection with Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom, beginning on shared fol. 89v and extending to fol. 148r. That adjacent work is essential whole-codex context but is not part of CME00060. Brown's Three Women of Liège later re-edits only the first three lives and likewise is not coextensive with CME00060.

extent.description: Four numbered Middle English source items are framed by a German editorial introduction and a separately structured closing compiler apology; the opening apology is nested inside the first numbered division. Encoding metrics are one DIV1, six DIV2, eight DIV3, seventy DIV4 (six Elizabeth chapters, thirty-eight Christina chapters, and twenty-six Marie chapters), 106 P, 224 NOTE, and 95 PB elements covering printed pp. 102-196.

content_parts.label: Horstmann's editorial frame and apparatus
content_parts.summary: German title, manuscript and source introduction, supplied headings, footnotes, textual observations, and signed Berlin close.

locators.value: introduction pp. 102-106; headings and notes throughout pp. 102-196

notes: This is a conceptual, noncontiguous editorial part; its notes are interleaved with the source texts.

content_parts.label: Þe Apologe of the compilour
content_parts.summary: The anonymous compiler-translator explains sense-for-sense translation from Latin and selective omission of difficult scriptural authorities.

locators.value: p. 107, shared with the opening of Elizabeth
at the opening of the fols. 1-12 Elizabeth item; exact ending not separately reported

notes: The XML nests this DIV3 inside the first numbered legend, but its translation policy frames the larger four-text group.

content_parts.label: Life of St. Elizabeth of Spalbeek
content_parts.summary: Philip of Clairvaux's eyewitness-oriented account of Elizabeth's embodied Passion devotion, translated into Middle English and divided by the edition into six thematic chapters.

locators.value: fols. 1r-12; final side unspecified by Horstmann

content_parts.label: Life of St. Christina Mirabilis
content_parts.summary: A Middle English translation of Thomas of Cantimpré's life of Christina the Astonishing, with a prologue and thirty-eight numbered chapters.

locators.value: fols. 12-26v; initial side unspecified by Horstmann

notes: Printed p. 134 / BSB canvas 148 is shared with the opening of Marie.

content_parts.label: Life of St. Marie d'Oignies
content_parts.summary: A Middle English translation of Jacques de Vitry's life of Marie d'Oignies, preceded by the English compiler's short prologue and arranged in two books of thirteen chapters each.

locators.value: 134-184; first explicit PB inside the division is 135 because p. 134 is shared
fols. 26v-76; final side unspecified by Horstmann

notes: Printed p. 184 / BSB canvas 198 is shared with the opening of the Catherine letter.

content_parts.label: Stefano Maconi's letter concerning St. Catherine of Siena
content_parts.summary: A Middle English translation of Maconi's letter to Thomas Antonii of Siena recounting Catherine's deeds and virtues; it is epistolary testimony rather than a conventional full vita.

locators.value: 184-195; first explicit PB inside the division is 185 because p. 184 is shared
fols. 76-89v; initial side unspecified by Horstmann

notes: Printed p. 195 / BSB canvas 209 is shared with the closing compiler apology. Brown's later edition excludes this work.

content_parts.label: A shorte Apologetik of þis englisshe compyloure
content_parts.summary: The compiler asks indulgence for stylistic variation and mixed northern and southern English, attributes faults to ignorance and obedient labor, and explains selected translation equivalents.

locators.value: 195-196; the only PB inside the division is 196 because p. 195 is shared
after the Catherine letter and before Seven Points on or near shared fol. 89v; exact lines unresolved

related_works.title: Vita S. Elizabethae de Spalbec
notes: Latin source life by Philip of Clairvaux for part:elizabeth; the precise physical Latin exemplar remains unidentified.

related_works.title: Vita Christinae Mirabilis
notes: Latin source life by Thomas of Cantimpré for part:christina.

related_works.title: Vita Mariae Oigniacensis
notes: Latin source life by Jacques de Vitry for part:marie; the English compiler intentionally omits the elaborate Latin proem.

related_works.title: Epistola de gestis et virtutibus S. Catherinae
notes: Stefano Maconi's Latin letter dated 26 October 1411; exact physical exemplar unknown.

related_works.title: The Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom
notes: The adjacent fifth work in MS. Douce 114 begins on shared fol. 89v and runs to fol. 148r. It is excluded from CME00060 but must remain in any complete-codex facsimile.

related_works.title: Three Women of Liège
notes: Brown's 2009 critical edition and commentary covers Elizabeth, Christina, and Marie, but not the Catherine letter or the complete Horstmann editorial frame.

evidence.locator: IDG; title and headnotes; DIV1-DIV4 hierarchy; PB, P, NOTE, HI; compiler apologies; signed close
evidence.summary: Establishes the encoded title, editor, language declaration, prose structure, exact printed page sequence, four numbered texts, compiler framing, chapter organization, source names, subjects, and structural counts.

evidence.locator: count(//DIV1), count(//DIV2), count(//DIV3), count(//DIV4), count(//P), count(//NOTE), count(//HI[@REND='italic']), count(//PB)
evidence.summary: Reproduces one DIV1, six DIV2, eight DIV3, seventy DIV4, 106 P, 224 NOTE, 3,442 italic HI, and 95 PB elements; the seventy chapter divisions break down as 6 + 38 + 26.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Prosalegenden: Die Legenden des MS. Douce 114,' Anglia 8 (1885): 102-196
evidence.locator: BSB canvases 116-210; especially introduction pp. 102-106 and source texts pp. 107-196
evidence.summary: Supports article identity, per-part pages and manuscript folios, source authorship, date of Maconi's letter, compiler statements, content summaries, language and form, editorial selection, and manuscript provenance claims as attributed to Horstmann.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries MMOL TEI record for MS. Douce 114, manuscript_4492
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark, five contents, named source authors for Elizabeth and Catherine, Middle English language, fifteenth-century English parchment codex, current extent and dimensions, and Beauvale provenance.

evidence.citation: Brian C. Vander Veen, The Vitae of Bodleian Library MS Douce 114 (PhD thesis, University of Nottingham, 2007)
evidence.summary: Supports the unique four-text boundary, physical and hand descriptions, cautions about Beauvale production, compiler translation and audience statements, Rutland and Central Midlands dialect discussion, and evidence that Douce 114 copies an earlier English exemplar.

evidence.citation: Brepols, Three Women of Liège product record
evidence.summary: Confirms the names and Latin biographers of the three Liège women, fifteenth-century Middle English translation, unique survival together in Douce 114, current critical-edition identity, scope, ISBNs, and access route.

evidence.citation: Karen Winstead, review of Jennifer N. Brown, Three Women of Liège, The Medieval Review 10.09.02 (2010)
evidence.summary: Supports first-quarter-fifteenth-century translation, first-half-fifteenth-century witness dating, earlier English-copy evidence, the Catherine letter's exclusion and possible later quire, and Horstmann's italic abbreviation expansions.

evidence.citation: Biblissima+, Oxford. Bodleian Library, MS. Douce 114, Q274556
evidence.summary: Supplies the grouping title Lives of Four Female Saints, its conflicting fols. 1r-87r locus, and the adjacent Seven Points locus fols. 89v-148r.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00060
evidence.summary: Binds descriptive metadata to the repository artifact, U-M encoding, Horstmann edition, Bodleian witness, complete and partial source scans, catalogs, rights evidence, and practical access routes.

open_questions.question: Where were the four Middle English translations produced and gathered, as distinct from the later Beauvale ownership of MS. Douce 114?
next_steps: Consult Brown's full introduction and later scholarship on the proposed Thurgarton and Beauvale contexts.
Keep the Rutland linguistic localization, Central Midlands written standard, Beauvale provenance, and any institutional production claim separate.
Compare scribal, quire, and dialect evidence in a whole-codex facsimile before assigning a narrower composition or copying place.
notes: Horstmann's Nottinghamshire title is explicitly conjectural and based largely on later provenance.

open_questions.question: Does the four-text group end on fol. 89v, as Horstmann reports, or fol. 87r, as Biblissima/JONAS reports, and where exactly does the closing compiler apology fall?
next_steps: Request a Bodleian item-level foliation confirmation and a crosswalk for the closing apology and Seven Points opening.
Inspect a complete-codex facsimile without cropping the shared fol. 89v boundary.
Retain the edition-reported fols. 1r-89v mapping until stronger item-level evidence resolves the conflict.

open_questions.question: Which Latin manuscripts and lost or unidentified earlier Middle English copies stand behind each surviving Douce 114 translation?
next_steps: Consult Brown's textual analysis of the first three lives and the relevant Latin manuscript census.
Research the Maconi letter's Latin transmission separately because Brown's edition excludes it.
Do not equate a close surviving Latin comparison manuscript with the translator's physical exemplar without transmission evidence.

notes: The manuscript's f. 89v boundary is shared with an excluded adjacent work and must remain visible in any full-codex surrogate.
The XML's italic markup preserves Horstmann's abbreviation-expansion typography; it cannot answer paleographic questions about the original manuscript without images.
The record remains partial because the exact manuscript boundary, translation exemplars, and production context are unresolved and no public whole-codex facsimile was found.
